registry_context.dimensions: Affective/Emotional, Anthropological/Structural, Spiritual/God-ward, Relational/Social, Volitional/Capacity, Moral/Conscience

synthesis_questions: How does the psuchikos/pneumatikos distinction in 1Co 2:14 and 15:44-46 map onto the spirit registry's own account of the pneuma — is the spirit registry's pneuma the same as 1Co 15:46's pneumatikon?
Is the soul without the Spirit (G5591 psuchikos) a fallen condition, a creaturely limitation, or both — and how does the spirit registry's data clarify this?
Does the spirit registry contain verses where pneuma and psuchē are used in explicit contrast, complementing or complicating the Pauline psuchikos/pneumatikos framework?
pointers.prose_note: G5591 psuchikos is the soul registry's most important cross-registry term. It establishes the soul as a mode of existence that can operate without the Spirit's dimension — the natural creaturely person (1Co 2:14), the natural body as opposed to the resurrection body (1Co 15:44), the earthly unspiritual wisdom of Jam 3:15 and Jude 19. In every use, psuchikos marks the lower register — not evil but incomplete. The soul is the creature's given reality; the Spirit is the eschatological completion. This distinction cannot be fully mapped from within the soul registry alone. The spirit registry (184) must establish what the pneuma IS before the soul's relationship to it can be synthesised. The eschatological trajectory (psuchikon raised as pneumatikon, 1Co 15:45) is among the most structurally significant statements in the NT anthropology and requires both registries to interpret.

synthesis_questions: Is the soul's appetite (H5315L ne.phesh) the same capacity as the desire vocabulary in registry 043, or does the soul-register add something the desire vocabulary alone does not capture?
If the soul is fundamentally appetitive — constitutively reaching toward something — does the desire registry confirm or complicate this structural hypothesis?
How does the soul's appetitive orientation connect to the will vocabulary — is willing (volitional) a higher-order function of the same appetitive soul, or a distinct capacity?
pointers.prose_note: H5315L ne.phesh:appetite is the sub-gloss most directly shared with the desire registry. The soul and desire may not be two things but one — the soul IS the desiring capacity. Deu 14:26 uses ne.phesh and ta.a.vah in synonymous parallelism: 'whatever your soul desires, whatever your appetite desires.' Ecc 6:7 — 'the appetite (ne.phesh) is not satisfied' — the soul's appetitive restlessness as its defining problem when wrongly directed. The structural hypothesis emerging from the full verse reading: the soul is the creature as appetitive — always reaching toward something, and the health of the soul is always the question of its objects. If the desire registry confirms this structure, the soul-as-appetite becomes a major organising category for the programme's anthropological framework.

synthesis_questions: Mat 10:28 introduces the soul as the dimension surviving bodily death and standing under God's ultimate authority — is this the fear registry's primary soul-verse, and does the fear registry's analysis of 'fear of God' depend on this soul-vulnerability?
Does the soul's vulnerability to ultimate threat (Mat 10:28) constitute the deepest ground for the fear vocabulary — i.e. is the rightly-ordered fear of God ultimately a soul-level reality?
pointers.prose_note: Mat 10:28 — 'do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell.' This verse is simultaneously in the soul registry (soul as the dimension surviving bodily death) and the fear registry (the ultimate object of fear is the one with authority over the soul). The soul is introduced here as what makes ultimate fear possible — the body can be destroyed by humans, but the soul's ultimate vulnerability belongs only to God. This may be the most theologically concentrated cross-registry verse in the programme: it establishes the soul as the locus of ultimate vulnerability AND the ground for rightly-ordered fear of God. The fear registry's analysis of reverential fear may depend structurally on the soul vocabulary.

synthesis_questions: Does the soul's capacity for being 'cast down' (Psa 42:5) constitute the primary OT vocabulary for grief, or does the grief registry document a distinct Hebrew vocabulary for grief that does not use ne.phesh?
The hope command in Psa 42:5 is addressed TO the soul — does the hope registry contain a corresponding pattern of hope being directed at or through the soul?
Lam 3:17 'my soul is bereft of peace' — is this verse in both the soul and peace registries, and does it function differently in each?
pointers.prose_note: H5315G ne.phesh connects across the Psalter's affective vocabulary to grief, hope, and peace registries. The soul is the site where grief is felt (cast down, Job 30:25), hope is commanded (Psa 42:5 — 'hope in God' addressed to the soul itself), and peace is lost or found (Lam 3:17, Heb 6:19). These connections are not incidental — the soul appears to be the affective centre that the grief, hope, and peace vocabularies are addressing from different angles. Session D should examine whether these registries form a coherent cluster around the soul's affective life.

synthesis_questions: G1374 dipsuchos appears in both the soul registry (182) and the mind registry (112). Is double-mindedness fundamentally a soul condition, a mind condition, or both — and does the distinction matter?
Does the will registry (173) document a corresponding pattern of divided will that maps onto the double-minded soul/mind vocabulary?
pointers.prose_note: G1374 dipsuchos (double-minded, Jam 1:8; 4:8) appears in both the soul and mind registries. The compound dipsuchos (two-souled) suggests the soul-register is primary — the division is at the soul's centre. But in the mind registry it appears alongside the Hebrew se.eph (divided, Ps 119:113) as a cross-testament pattern of inner division. Session D should examine whether inner division (double-mindedness, divided soul, wavering mind) is a cross-registry theme that connects soul, mind, and will into a coherent pattern of inner fragmentation versus integration.

synthesis_questions: The use of soul-vocabulary for God (Exo 31:17 na.phash, Isa 42:1 ne.phesh, Jer 32:41 ne.phesh) raises the question: is the soul vocabulary primarily creaturely, and its application to God purely analogical, or does the biblical text intend something stronger?

Does any other registry in the programme document comparable analogical use of inner-being vocabulary for God — and if so, does a pattern emerge about which dimensions of the inner being the biblical text is willing to attribute to God?

The Exo 31:17 verse (God refreshed after creation) is embedded in Sabbath theology. Does the Sabbath provide a theological framework for understanding divine soul-vocabulary that connects to the soul's somatic groundedness (rest, refreshment, restoration)?
pointers.prose_note: Three verses apply soul-vocabulary to God: Exo 31:17 (God refreshed — na.phash, the somatic refreshment verb), Isa 42:1 (God's soul delights in the servant), Jer 32:41 (God's whole-soul commitment to restoration). PH2-182-003 flags Exo 31:17 for exegetical study. This is not merely a curiosity — it reveals that the soul vocabulary is the available language for expressing divine inner states analogous to human inner states. The soul vocabulary is the anthropological medium through which divine pathos is communicated. Session D should examine whether this analogical use of soul-language for God is consistent across registries, and what it implies for the programme's account of the relationship between the human inner being and the divine character.

synthesis_questions: 1Th 5:23 preserves the whole person as spirit, soul, and body — does the heart registry (183) sit within or alongside the soul in this triad, or does heart occupy a distinct position?

Heb 4:12 distinguishes soul and spirit as the Word distinguishes joints and marrow — does the spirit registry's analysis confirm that the distinction is real but requires divine revelation to perceive?
The soul's place in the triad (between spirit and body in 1Th 5:23) — is this an ontological hierarchy, a functional description, or a rhetorical intensifier for 'the whole person'?
pointers.prose_note: The soul's place in the spirit-soul-body triad (1Th 5:23, Heb 4:12) cannot be established from the soul registry alone. The soul registry establishes what the soul IS — the animated person, constitutively appetitive, relationally oriented. But its relationship to spirit (above) and body (below) requires both the spirit registry (184) and the heart registry (183) to map with precision. The critical verses (Heb 4:12 — soul and spirit distinguished by the Word; 1Th 5:23 — the whole person preserved as triad; 1Co 15:44-46 — the eschatological transformation from psuchikon to pneumatikon) are all multi-registry verses. This is the most important cluster of cross-registry dependencies in the C01 group.

synthesis_questions: The soul-loss/soul-finding paradox (Mat 10:39 and parallels) inverts the soul's natural self-preservation instinct — is this the same dynamic as repentance (metanoeō = change of mind/orientation), or a distinct but related transformation?
Does the repentance registry document the soul-loss paradox as a repentance-adjacent movement, or is it better categorised as a discipleship/self-denial dynamic?
pointers.prose_note: The soul-loss/soul-finding paradox (Mat 10:39, 16:25; Mar 8:35; Luk 9:24, 17:33; Joh 12:25) structurally parallels repentance: both involve the release of the person's default orientation (self-preservation / self-justification) in order to receive a new orientation. The soul that clings to itself operates like the unrepentant person — preserving the old self at the cost of the new. This connection is not direct enough to assert as a synthesis claim, but Session D should examine whether the soul-loss paradox is best understood as a soul-level expression of the same transformation dynamic that repentance documents at the mind/cognitive level.
